name: Dokkaebi
description: Dokkaebi, often translated as goblins, are mischievous spirits or deities in Korean folklore. Unlike Western goblins, Dokkaebi are not inherently evil but are more akin to tricksters, possessing supernatural powers and using them to interact with humans. They are known for their playful antics, rewarding good deeds and punishing the wicked. Dokkaebi are often depicted as grotesque figures with horns, sharp teeth, and a single leg or eye, though their appearance varies widely across different tales and regions. They enjoy games, feasts, and causing minor disturbances. They are closely associated with the natural world, particularly forests and abandoned structures. Dokkaebi gain their power from inanimate objects imbued with human blood or spirit, such as discarded household tools. Their interactions with humans often involve tests of wit, strength, or morality, with the outcome determining whether the human receives fortune or misfortune. While generally harmless, they can be dangerous if angered or disrespected. Dokkaebi stories serve as moral tales, highlighting the importance of honesty, kindness, and respect for the supernatural world.
subtitle: Mischievous spirits of Korean folklore
appearance: Dokkaebi appearance varies greatly, but they are often depicted as grotesque and fearsome creatures. Common features include horns, sharp teeth, a large head, and sometimes only one leg or eye. They can be tall and imposing or short and stout. Their skin color is said to range from red and blue to green and black. They often wear traditional Korean clothing, such as hanbok, but torn or tattered. Their overall appearance is meant to be unsettling yet also somewhat comical.
habitat: Dokkaebi are associated with wild and abandoned places, such as forests, mountains, caves, abandoned houses, and graveyards. They are believed to reside in areas where human activity is minimal and the natural world holds sway. Specific locations mentioned in folklore include dark forests, lonely mountain passes, and crumbling buildings that have been left to decay.
behavior: Dokkaebi are generally mischievous and playful, enjoying pranks and games. They are known to test humans through riddles, contests of strength, or moral dilemmas. They reward those who are honest, kind, and respectful, while punishing the wicked and arrogant. Dokkaebi are also fond of feasts and celebrations, often gathering in large numbers to dance, sing, and make merry. While not inherently evil, they can be dangerous if angered or provoked.

keyMyths.title: The Dokkaebi and the Honest Man
keyMyths.description: A Dokkaebi tries to trick an honest man, but the man's unwavering honesty prevents the Dokkaebi from succeeding. The Dokkaebi, impressed by the man's virtue, rewards him with riches and good fortune.

keyMyths.title: The Dokkaebi's Magic Club
keyMyths.description: A woodcutter finds a Dokkaebi's magic club, which can conjure anything he desires. However, he soon becomes greedy and abuses its power, leading to his downfall and the loss of the club.

sources.author: Zong In-sob
sources.description: A collection of Korean folktales, including several stories featuring Dokkaebi.
